ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_03301710 covers lime juice as a category; no specific Thai lime zest juice entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers lime juice generically. No specific Thai preparation code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229961001 covers lime juice as a substance; used as closest parent for this Thai lime zest juice preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one glass) of freshly prepared lime zest juice, as typically served in Thai restaurants and juice bars.

nutrient_source.title: Lime juice, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167660)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy data for raw lime juice (FDC ID 167660). The 'Lively Lime Zest' product is a freshly squeezed lime juice with zest, served at a Thai restaurant in Singapore. Values are adapted from raw lime juice as the closest defensible match; minor zest contribution to micronutrients is noted but does not materially alter macronutrient profile per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for Thai lime zest juice. GI of 30 is estimated based on fresh citrus juice (lemon/lime juice) analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021). Citrus juices with low sugar content and high organic acid content consistently show GI in the 25–40 range. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 21.0) / 100 = 6.3, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving ≈ 21.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (2 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Lime zest juice is very low in sodium (approximately 2 mg per 100 g) and contains potassium (117 mg per 100 g), making it a suitable beverage choice for individuals managing hypertension; however, added sugar or salt in restaurant preparations should be confirmed and minimised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and moderate natural sugar content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated glycemic index of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of 6.3 per 250 ml serving, lime zest juice poses a low glycemic burden; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should nonetheless confirm that no added sugar is included in the restaurant preparation, as sweetened versions can significantly raise the glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, high vitamin C, and antioxidant flavonoids from lime zest
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lime zest juice is virtually fat-free (0.1 g fat per 100 g, 0.01 g saturated fat) and provides approximately 30 mg vitamin C per 100 g along with flavonoids from the zest, which may support endothelial function; it is a heart-friendly beverage choice when consumed without added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lime Zest Juice (marketed as 'Lively Lime Zest') is a freshly prepared citrus beverage made from squeezed lime juice combined with grated or pressed lime zest, served chilled. In Thai culinary tradition, lime (มะนาว, manao) is a foundational flavour ingredient used in beverages, dressings, and sauces. The drink delivers a sharp, tangy, and slightly bitter citrus flavour profile with aromatic top notes from the essential oils in the zest. It is minimally processed — typically squeezed to order — and may be served plain or lightly sweetened. Nutritionally, lime juice is low in calories (approximately 25 kcal per 100 g), very low in fat (0.1 g), and provides moderate carbohydrate (8.4 g per 100 g) primarily as natural sugars. It is a notable source of vitamin C (approximately 30 mg per 100 g), supporting immune function and collagen synthesis. The lime zest fraction contributes flavonoids and volatile aromatic compounds. With a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of 6.3 per standard 250 ml serving, it is suitable for individuals monitoring blood glucose. The very low sodium content (2 mg per 100 g) and meaningful potassium content (117 mg per 100 g) make it appropriate for hypertension management. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. In Singapore, this beverage is served at Thai restaurants and health-focused juice bars, often positioned as a refreshing and health-conscious drink option.
common_user_queries: Is lime juice good for diabetes?
Is lime juice good for blood pressure?
Lively Lime Zest field survey nutrition

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for 'Lively Lime Zest' exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw lime juice data (FDC ID 167660), which is the closest defensible match. GI is estimated from analogous citrus juice data in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact restaurant preparation (sugar addition, dilution ratio, zest quantity) is unknown.
notes: 1. The input record classified this food under 'Vegetables' food group and 'Thai' cuisine; the food group has been corrected to 'Beverages' as lime zest juice is a beverage product. 2. Cuisine tag 'Thai' is retained as the serving restaurant (The Upper House - Funan) lists Thai cuisine. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy source for raw lime juice; naturally zero as this is a plant-derived citrus juice with no industrial hydrogenation. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived beverage. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived beverage containing no animal products. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; naturally zero in citrus juice. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.01), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.03) are reported at trace levels per USDA SR Legacy. 8. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on citrus juice data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Thai lime zest juice was found in approved sources. 9. The food_name has been normalised from the promotional menu name 'Lively Lime Zest' to the canonical English name 'Lime Zest Juice'; the original menu name is preserved in aliases. 10. Thai mandatory local-language name (น้ำมะนาว) is present per G6 multilingual compliance rules for Thai cuisine.
